To find the value of \(\alpha\), we need to solve the given equation for \(\sin \alpha\) or \(\cos \alpha\). However, it's more convenient to solve for \(\tan \alpha\) first.

Let's start by finding the numerator and denominator separately:

Numerator:
\[\sin ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right)-\sin ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right)-\sin 195^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\]

Using the identity \(\sin ^{2} x = 1 - \cos ^{2} x\), we can rewrite the terms as:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) + \sin 195^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right) \\
= \cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - 1 + \sin 195^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Now, we can use the identity \(\cos \left(a-b\right) = \cos a \cos b + \sin a \sin b\) to simplify the last term:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\sin 195^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right) &= \sin \left(180^{\circ}-15^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right) \\
&= -\cos 15^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right) + \sin 15^{\circ} \sin \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Substitute this back into the numerator:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - 1 + \sin 195^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right) \\
= \cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - 1 + \left(-\cos 15^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right) + \sin 15^{\circ} \sin \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right) \\
= \left(\cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - \cos 15^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right) - \sin 15^{\circ} \sin \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Denominator:

\[
\cos ^{2}\left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right)-\cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right)+\sin 15^{\circ} \sin \left(75^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)
\]

Using the same identity \(\cos \left(a-b\right) = \cos a \cos b + \sin a \sin b\), we can simplify the first two terms:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\cos ^{2}\left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right)-\cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) &= \cos ^{2}\left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - 2 \cos \left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right) \cos \left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) \\
&= \left(\cos ^{2}\left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - 2 \cos 15^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Now, we can substitute the simplified numerator and denominator back into the original equation:

\[
\frac{\left(\cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - \cos 15^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right) - \sin 15^{\circ} \sin \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)}{\left(\cos ^{2}\left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - 2 \cos 15^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right)} = -1
\]

To solve for \(\tan \alpha\), we can cross-multiply:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(\cos ^{2}\left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - 2 \cos 15^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right) \left(-\sin 15^{\circ} \sin \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right) \\
=\left(\left(\cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) - \cos 15^{\circ} \cos \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right) - \sin 15^{\circ} \sin \left(165^{\circ}-2 \alpha\right)\right) \left(\cos ^{2}\left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right) + \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) \right) \\
\end{aligned}
\]

Expanding the product:

\[

+\cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) \cos ^{2}\left(225^{\circ}+\alpha\right) +\cos ^{2}\left(135^{\circ}-\alpha\right) \cos ^{2}\left(210^{\circ}-\alpha\right) \\
